sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, LEFT, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 76, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player (green, 2x3) at rows 41-43, cols 32-35 - Three blue squares (2x2 each) in dark gray frames (3x3 each) arranged in a triangle - Blue square 3 frame blocks upward path (rows 36-39, cols 32-35) - UP movement moves player up 4 rows when clear; blocked when obstacle in path - Goal unclear - likely navigate to collect/interact with blue squares
- Plan: Navigate to remaining blue squares and press SPACE to remove their frames.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 75 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After SPACE, LEFT, SPACE:
1. SPACE: white shrunk from 23-27 to just 23, middle frame (G) reappeared at 24-27
2. LEFT: player moved from 20-22 to 17-19, white moved from 23 to 16. The left frame changed from color c to G! And the right frame and middle frame MERGED into one big frame at cols 24-31 (24 pixels, color c).
3. SPACE: white expanded from 16 to 12-16, covering the left frame (which was G). The left frame disappeared.
Key observations:
- When I moved LEFT, the left frame changed color from c to G
- The middle and right frames merged into one big frame (cols 24-31, 24 pixels)
- The white square now covers cols 12-16 (expanded leftward)
- The left frame (G) at cols 12-15 is now covered by white
So the mechanic seems to be:
- The white square can expand in the direction OPPOSITE to the player's movement
- When player moves LEFT, white square is on the LEFT side (it swapped sides!)
- The frame that the player moves toward changes color to G (becomes "active")
- SPACE expands the white square to cover the active frame
Wait, let me reconsider. After the LEFT move:
- White is at col 16 (LEFT of player at 17-19)
- Left frame is at cols 12-15, color G (changed from c)
- The middle and right frames merged at cols 24-31
So the white square is now on the LEFT of the player! It switched sides when the player moved LEFT.
And when I pressed SPACE, the white square expanded LEFT to cover cols 12-16, covering the left frame.
This is a key insight: the white square switches to the side the player moved toward, and can expand in that direction.
Now the state is:
- White square at cols 12-16 (expanded, covering left frame)
- Player at cols 17-19
- Big merged frame at cols 24-31 (color c, 24 pixels)
- Blue squares at cols 13-14, 25-26, 29-30
The left frame is covered. The middle and right frames merged. I need to cover the merged frame too.
To do that, I need to move RIGHT (toward the merged frame), which should make the white square switch to the RIGHT side and the merged frame change to G. Then SPACE to expand and cover it.
But wait, let me first check: did covering the left frame "free" the blue square inside? The blue at cols 13-14 is still blue. So covering doesn't free the blue square immediately.
Let me try: RIGHT to move toward the merged frame, then SPACE to cover it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White square now on LEFT of player, expanded to cover left frame
# Player at cols 17-19, white at cols 12-16
# Merged frame at cols 24-31
# Need to cover the merged frame

# First shrink white, then move RIGHT toward merged frame
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"After SPACE: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Move RIGHT toward merged frame
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"After RIGHT: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# SPACE to expand and cover
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"After SPACE: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
